aliases: deep-fried potato with truffle oil and parmesan

english: deep-fried potato with truffle oil and parmesan

region_of_origin: Western (Europe / North America); widely served globally as a restaurant side dish

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general french fries entry (FOODON_00002694). Truffle fries are a flavoured variant with truffle oil and parmesan; no dedicated FoodOn term exists for this preparation. 'closest_parent' match type used as it's a specific variant of a general FoodOn term.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ covers fried potato products. Truffle oil and parmesan seasoning are not captured in this code; 'closest_parent' match type is the most appropriate as it's a specific variant of a general FoodEx2 term.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for truffle fries or flavoured french fries identified in the clinical terminology. Omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant side serving of truffle fries is approximately 120 g, equivalent to a small basket or side plate portion.

nutrient_source.title: Truffle Fries — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F14060018 is sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. Core macros, dietary fibre, sodium, calcium, and iron are taken directly from the HPB record. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, and cholesterol are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for french fries (fried in oil) as HPB does not separately report these items; this adaptation is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 75 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database for french fries (deep-fried potato). Truffle oil and parmesan seasoning do not materially alter the GI of the potato base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × net carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (75 × 39.48) / 100 = 29.6 g GL per 120 g serving (net carbs per 120 g = (36.6 − 3.7) × 1.2 = 39.48 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 366 mg/100 g from added salt, parmesan cheese, and seasoning; deep-fried preparation adds caloric density
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid truffle fries due to 366 mg sodium per 100 g; if consumed, request reduced salt and parmesan, and restrict portion to no more than 60–80 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~75) combined with 36.6 g carbohydrate and 15.5 g fat per 100 g creates a high glycaemic and caloric burden
type2_diabetes.guidance: Truffle fries are unsuitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to a high GI (~75), high carbohydrate load (36.6 g/100 g), and high fat content that sustains postprandial hyperglycaemia; oven-baked potato with skin is a lower-GI alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 15.5 g/100 g including saturated fat ~2.1 g and estimated trans fat ~0.1 g; high sodium 366 mg/100 g; deep-fried preparation generates oxidised lipids and AGEs
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Truffle fries present a high cardiovascular risk due to elevated total fat, estimated trans fat from frying, high sodium, and pro-inflammatory advanced glycation end products generated by deep-frying; consumption should be minimised in CVD patients.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation generates AGEs; potato is a nightshade (solanine); estimated trans fat ~0.1 g/100 g is pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Truffle fries are contraindicated for rheumatoid arthritis management: deep-frying generates AGEs that directly worsen joint inflammation, potato is a nightshade that may trigger inflammatory responses, and estimated trans fat is strongly pro-inflammatory.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Truffle fries are deep-fried potato strips seasoned with truffle oil and topped with grated parmesan cheese, served as a premium side dish in Western-style restaurants and bistros worldwide, including Singapore. The dish is a flavoured variant of classic french fries, distinguished by the earthy, umami-rich aroma of truffle oil and the salty, nutty flavour of parmesan. The texture is crispy on the outside and soft inside. Per 100 g, truffle fries provide 304 kcal, 4.5 g protein, 15.5 g fat, and 36.6 g carbohydrate with 3.7 g dietary fibre and 366 mg sodium. The high glycemic index (~75) and high carbohydrate load make this dish unsuitable for Type 2 Diabetes and cardiovascular disease management. The sodium content from added salt and parmesan raises concern for hypertension. Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) and oxidised lipids, which are pro-inflammatory and relevant for rheumatoid arthritis and cardiovascular disease. Truffle fries are vegetarian but not vegan due to parmesan. They are commonly consumed as a side dish or snack in casual dining and fine-dining settings.
common_user_queries: How many calories in truffle fries?
Is truffle fries high in sodium?
What is the GI of french fries?
Can I eat truffle fries if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F14060018, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, and cholesterol are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for deep-fried french fries as HPB does not separately report these fatty acid fractions for this item; confidence remains high for the HPB-sourced fields and medium for the USDA-estimated fatty acid fractions.
notes: 1. sugar_g is set to -1: the input record reported sugar as -1.0 (sentinel value indicating data not available). Sugar data for this specific truffle fries preparation is not separately reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for F14060018. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for french fries reports sugar at approximately 0.3 g/100 g but this value was not confirmed in the HPB source; -1 is retained to preserve source fidelity. 2. saturated_fat_g (2.1 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (6.8 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (5.2 g), trans_fat_g (0.1 g), potassium_mg (480.0), cholesterol_mg (4.0) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for french fries (fried in vegetable oil) as HPB does not separately report these values for F14060018. 3. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific truffle fries preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database for F14060018, and USDA SR Legacy values for plain french fries were not applied to avoid overstating precision for a seasoned restaurant preparation. 4. GI of 75 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for deep-fried french fries; truffle oil and parmesan do not materially alter the GI of the potato base. Glycemic load per 120 g serving = 75 × 39.48 / 100 = 29.6 g. 5. This is a Western dish; multilingual naming requirements (Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, Vietnamese) do not apply. No local-language names are required or populated. 6. Dietary tag 'plant_based' from input has been removed as parmesan cheese is an animal product; 'vegetarian' and 'high_fibre' are retained.
